nodes.title: Interpret the derivative as a rate of change
nodes.brief: Read dy/dx as the rate of change of y with respect to x, and chain rates through a common variable.
nodes.quote: represents the rate of change of *y* with respect to *x*
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — dy/dx is the rate of change of y with respect to x.
For y = f(x), the derivative dy/dx measures how fast y changes per unit change in x; evaluated at x0 it gives the instantaneous rate there. A positive value means y is increasing, negative means decreasing.

PART 2 — Chain rates through a common variable (related rates).
When several quantities vary with time t, link their rates: dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt). This is the basis of related-rates problems (expanding circle: dA/dt = (dA/dr)(dr/dt) = 2 pi r dr/dt). Track which rate is given and which is wanted.
keyMoves: Read dy/dx as how fast y changes per unit change in x
Evaluate at a point for the instantaneous rate
Link rates through time: dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt)
misconceptions: Treating dy/dx as a fixed number rather than a rate that can vary
Forgetting the chain link when both quantities depend on time

nodes.title: Define increasing and decreasing on an interval
nodes.brief: Define f increasing on I if x1 < x2 implies f(x1) <= f(x2), decreasing if f(x1) >= f(x2).
nodes.quote: increasing on I if  $x_1 < x_2$  in I  $\Rightarrow f(x_1) \le f(x_2)$  for all
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Increasing: bigger input gives a bigger-or-equal output.
f is increasing on an interval I if, for all x1 < x2 in I, f(x1) <= f(x2). f is decreasing if x1 < x2 implies f(x1) >= f(x2). Increasing/decreasing describes how the OUTPUT orders as the input grows.

PART 2 — Strict versions use strict inequalities.
With f(x1) < f(x2) the function is strictly increasing; with f(x1) > f(x2), strictly decreasing. Note 'increasing' is NOT the same as 'positive' — a function can be negative everywhere yet increasing (e.g. y = x for x < 0).
keyMoves: Take any two points x1 < x2 in the interval
Compare f(x1) and f(x2)
Classify as (strictly) increasing or decreasing by the inequality direction
misconceptions: Confusing increasing (output grows) with the function being positive
Mixing up strict and non-strict versions

nodes.title: Use the sign of f'(x) to test monotonicity
nodes.brief: Apply: f is increasing where f'(x) >= 0 and decreasing where f'(x) <= 0.
nodes.quote: *f* is increasing in [*a*,*b*] if  $f'(x) \ge 0$  for each
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The sign of f'(x) determines increasing/decreasing.
For f continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b): f is increasing where f'(x) >= 0 and decreasing where f'(x) <= 0. If f'(x) > 0 throughout, f is strictly increasing; if f'(x) < 0 throughout, strictly decreasing.

PART 2 — Find intervals by solving the derivative inequality.
To find where f increases/decreases, compute f'(x), then solve f'(x) > 0 and f'(x) < 0. Example: f(x) = x^2 has f'(x) = 2x, so f decreases on (-inf, 0) and increases on (0, inf). The key error is using the sign of f(x) instead of f'(x).
keyMoves: Compute f'(x)
Find where f'(x) >= 0 (increasing) and f'(x) <= 0 (decreasing)
Report the intervals of increase and decrease
misconceptions: Using the sign of f(x) instead of f'(x)
Forgetting the continuity/differentiability hypotheses

nodes.title: Define maximum and minimum value on an interval
nodes.brief: f has a maximum value at c if f(c) > f(x) for all x in I; a minimum if f(c) < f(x).
nodes.quote: f is said to have a maximum value in I, if there exists a point c in I such that f(c) > f(x), for all  $x \in I$ .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Maximum/minimum VALUE is f(c), attained at a point c.
f has a maximum value in I if some point c has f(c) >= f(x) for all x in I; then f(c) is the maximum value and c is the point of maximum. Similarly a minimum value f(c) satisfies f(c) <= f(x) for all x. A max or min value is called an extreme value.

PART 2 — Distinguish the point c from the value f(c).
The point c is WHERE the extreme occurs; the value f(c) is the extreme value itself. Do not confuse them. Also, not every function on an interval attains a maximum or minimum (e.g. f(x) = x on an open interval).
keyMoves: Look for a point c whose value beats all others in I (maximum) or is below all others (minimum)
Report f(c) as the maximum/minimum value
Note an extreme value is either a maximum or a minimum value
misconceptions: Confusing the point c with the value f(c)
Assuming every function on an interval attains a maximum/minimum

nodes.title: Define local maxima and minima
nodes.brief: Recognise a local maximum/minimum at an interior point c where f(c) is greatest/least nearby.
nodes.quote: The value f(c) is called the *local maximum value* of f.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Local extrema compare f(c) with NEARBY values only.
At an interior point c, if f(c) is greater than f at all points in some small interval (c-h, c+h), then c is a local maximum and f(c) is the local maximum value. If f(c) is less than all nearby values, c is a local minimum. Only a small neighbourhood matters.

PART 2 — Local is not the same as absolute.
A local maximum need not be the largest value over the whole domain — it is just the largest NEARBY. A function can have several local maxima/minima. Look for them at interior points, not at the end points of the interval.
keyMoves: Restrict attention to a small neighbourhood of an interior point c
Compare f(c) with nearby values
Classify c as a local maximum or local minimum accordingly
misconceptions: Confusing local extrema (nearby comparison) with absolute extrema (whole domain)
Looking for local extrema at end points rather than interior points

nodes.title: Use that a local extremum forces f'(c) = 0 or non-differentiability
nodes.brief: Apply Theorem 2: at a local maxima/minima, either f'(c) = 0 or f is not differentiable at c.
nodes.quote: If f has a local maxima or a local minima at x = c, then either f'(c) = 0 or f is not differentiable at c.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Local extrema occur only where f'(c) = 0 or f' fails to exist.
If f has a local maximum or minimum at an interior point c, then either f'(c) = 0 or f is not differentiable at c. So you search for local extrema ONLY at such points — they are the only candidates.

PART 2 — The converse is FALSE.
f'(c) = 0 does NOT guarantee a local extremum. Counterexample: f(x) = x^3 has f'(0) = 0 but x=0 is neither a max nor a min — it is a point of inflexion. So you must test each candidate, not assume f'(c)=0 means an extremum.
keyMoves: Search for local extrema only where f'(c) = 0 or f' fails to exist
Do NOT conclude an extremum just because f'(c) = 0
Remember x^3 at 0 as the counterexample (point of inflexion)
misconceptions: Believing every point with f'(c) = 0 is a local extremum
Ignoring points where the derivative does not exist

nodes.title: Identify the critical points of a function
nodes.brief: Find critical points: where f'(x) = 0 or f is not differentiable.
nodes.quote: either f'(c) = 0 or f is not differentiable is called a *critical point* of f.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Critical points: f'(c) = 0 OR f not differentiable at c.
A critical point is any domain point where the derivative is zero or fails to exist. Find them by solving f'(x) = 0 AND noting points where f' does not exist (e.g. corners). Both sources count.

PART 2 — Critical points are the only candidates for local extrema.
By Fermat, every local extremum is a critical point — so finding critical points is the first step in any optimisation. But not every critical point is an extremum (x^3 at 0). On a closed interval, the candidates for absolute extrema are the critical points TOGETHER WITH the end points.
keyMoves: Solve f'(x) = 0 for stationary points
Add points where f is not differentiable
Treat all of these as the candidate critical points
misconceptions: Forgetting non-differentiable points (e.g. corners) as critical points
Assuming every critical point is automatically an extremum

nodes.title: Classify critical points by the first derivative test
nodes.brief: Use the sign change of f' across a critical point to decide maxima, minima or inflexion.
nodes.quote: Let f be continuous at a critical point c in I.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Inspect the sign of f' just left and just right of c.
At a critical point c, check the sign of f'(x) immediately before and after c. Sign change + to - means a local maximum. Sign change - to + means a local minimum. No sign change means neither (a point of inflexion).

PART 2 — Look at the sign AROUND c, not at c itself.
f'(c) is 0 (or undefined) at the critical point, so its value there tells you nothing — you must test points just to the left and right. A handy memory: f' going from rising-permitting (+) to falling-permitting (-) is a peak (max).
keyMoves: Find the critical points c
Check the sign of f'(x) just left and just right of c
+ to -: local maxima; - to +: local minima; no change: point of inflexion
misconceptions: Looking only at f' exactly at c (where it is 0) rather than the sign change around it
Calling a no-sign-change point an extremum

nodes.title: Classify critical points by the second derivative test
nodes.brief: At f'(c) = 0, use the sign of f''(c) to decide local maxima (<0) or minima (>0).
nodes.quote: x = c is a point of local maxima if f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) < 0
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — At f'(c) = 0, the sign of f''(c) classifies the point.
If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) < 0, then c is a local MAXIMUM (curve concave down, a peak). If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) > 0, then c is a local MINIMUM (concave up, a valley). Memory aid: negative second derivative = frown = maximum.

PART 2 — f''(c) = 0 is inconclusive.
If f''(c) = 0 the second derivative test fails — fall back on the first derivative test (sign change of f'). Do not swap the signs: f''(c) > 0 gives a MINIMUM, not a maximum.
keyMoves: Find c with f'(c) = 0
Compute f''(c): negative gives local maxima, positive gives local minima
If f''(c) = 0, revert to the first derivative test
misconceptions: Swapping the signs (thinking f''(c) > 0 gives a maximum)
Forgetting the test is inconclusive when f''(c) = 0

nodes.title: Define absolute (global) maximum and minimum
nodes.brief: Recognise the absolute maximum/minimum as the greatest/least value of f over the whole interval.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Absolute (global) extrema compare against ALL values on the interval.
The absolute maximum value is the GREATEST value of f over the whole interval; the absolute minimum is the LEAST. Unlike local extrema (which only beat nearby points), absolute extrema beat every other value on the interval.

PART 2 — They can occur at interior critical points OR at end points.
The absolute max/min may be at an interior critical point or at an end point a or b of the interval. So end points must be checked. An absolute extremum need not coincide with any local extremum.
keyMoves: Compare values over the entire interval, not just locally
Identify the single greatest value (absolute maximum) and least value (absolute minimum)
Remember end points are candidates too
misconceptions: Equating absolute extrema with local extrema
Ignoring end points when seeking absolute extrema

nodes.title: Use that a continuous function on [a,b] attains its extrema
nodes.brief: Apply Theorem 5: a continuous function on a closed interval attains both its absolute maximum and minimum.
nodes.quote: Then f has the absolute maximum value and f attains it at least once in I.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — A continuous function on a CLOSED interval attains both extrema.
The Extreme Value Theorem: if f is continuous on a closed interval [a, b], then f has an absolute maximum value and an absolute minimum value, each attained at least once in [a, b]. So the search for absolute extrema is guaranteed to succeed.

PART 2 — The closed interval and continuity are both essential.
On an OPEN interval (or with a discontinuity), this guarantee can fail — e.g. f(x) = x on (0, 1) attains neither its sup nor its inf. Always check the interval is closed and f is continuous before invoking the theorem.
keyMoves: Check the function is continuous on the closed interval [a, b]
Conclude both absolute maximum and absolute minimum exist and are attained
Note the guarantee can fail on an open interval
misconceptions: Applying the guarantee to an open interval
Thinking the extrema might not be attained even when f is continuous on [a, b]

nodes.title: Find absolute extrema of a function on a closed interval
nodes.brief: Apply the working rule: evaluate f at all critical points and at the end points, then pick the greatest and least.
nodes.quote: Step 1: Find all critical points of f in the interval, i.e., find points x where either f'(x) = 0 or f is not differentiable.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The working rule: critical points AND end points, then compare values.
To find absolute extrema of f on a closed interval [a, b]: Step 1, find all critical points in (a, b) (where f'(x) = 0 or f' does not exist). Step 2, evaluate f at every critical point AND at both end points a and b. Step 3, the LARGEST of these values is the absolute maximum, the SMALLEST is the absolute minimum.

PART 2 — Compare actual values; do not skip the end points.
The key is to compare the actual function VALUES, not to use a derivative test. The two common errors are forgetting to evaluate f at the end points, and using the second derivative test (which finds local extrema) instead of directly comparing values. This capstone combines critical points and the EVT guarantee for optimisation.
keyMoves: List all critical points in [a, b] (f'(x) = 0 or f' not defined)
Evaluate f at every critical point and at both end points a and b
Pick the greatest value (absolute maximum) and least value (absolute minimum)
misconceptions: Forgetting to evaluate f at the end points
Using the second derivative test instead of comparing actual values for absolute extrema
